exercises.title: Calculate the correct HikariCP pool size
exercises.instructions:
Calculate the recommended maximum-pool-size for each scenario:

1. Server: 4-core CPU, SSD storage (spindle=1)
2. Server: 8-core CPU, 5-disk RAID HDD (spindle=5)
3. Server: 16-core CPU, SSD (spindle=1)
4. 2 Spring Boot instances sharing one 4-core PostgreSQL server (SSD)
5. Why should you NOT set pool size to 500 on a 4-core server?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. 4-core SSD: (4×2)+1 = 9 connections
2. 8-core RAID-5 HDD: (8×2)+5 = 21 connections
3. 16-core SSD: (16×2)+1 = 33 connections
4. 2 instances sharing 4-core DB: total=(4×2)+1=9; per instance=9/2≈5 connections each
5. 500 connections on 4-core: DB server has 4 CPUs.
   500 concurrent queries = 500 threads competing for 4 CPU cores.
   Massive context switching overhead slows ALL queries.
   Optimal: 9 connections (well below CPU saturation).
   Too many connections = slower, not faster.

test_cases.expected:
1. 4-core SSD: (4×2)+1 = 9 connections
2. 8-core RAID-5 HDD: (8×2)+5 = 21 connections
3. 16-core SSD: (16×2)+1 = 33 connections
4. 2 instances sharing 4-core DB: total=(4×2)+1=9; per instance=9/2≈5 connections each
5. 500 connections on 4-core: DB server has 4 CPUs.
   500 concurrent queries = 500 threads competing for 4 CPU cores.
   Massive context switching overhead slows ALL queries.
   Optimal: 9 connections (well below CPU saturation).
   Too many connections = slower, not faster.

hints: Formula: (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count
SSD has 1 effective spindle; HDD RAID-5 = number of disks
Multiple app instances: divide total pool by instance count

exercises.title: Read EXPLAIN ANALYZE output — identify the problem
exercises.scenario: Interpret EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify whether an index is being used.
exercises.instructions:
For each EXPLAIN ANALYZE output snippet, state: (1) is an index being used, (2) what is the problem or conclusion:

```
A: Seq Scan on payments (cost=0..4892341)
   Filter: (merchant_id = 'M001')
   Rows Removed by Filter: 49999108
   Execution Time: 9823.4ms

B: Index Scan using idx_payments_merchant ON payments
   Index Cond: (merchant_id = 'M001')
   Execution Time: 0.8ms

C: Index Only Scan using idx_payments_cover ON payments
   Index Cond: (merchant_id = 'M001')
   Heap Fetches: 0
   Execution Time: 0.2ms

D: Seq Scan on payments
   Filter: (LOWER(email) = 'aisha@test.com')
   Rows Removed by Filter: 9999999
   Execution Time: 3421ms
```
hints: Seq Scan + Rows Removed: scanning entire table — missing index
Index Only Scan + Heap Fetches: 0 — best, covering index
Function in WHERE (LOWER(col)): regular index not used; need expression index

exercises.title: Write indexes as Flyway migration
exercises.scenario: Database indexes are added as Flyway migration SQL files.
exercises.instructions:
Write V5__add_performance_indexes.sql for the orders table:
1. Single index on user_id
2. Composite index on (status, created_at DESC) for active order queries
3. Composite index on (merchant_id, status) for merchant dashboard
4. Partial index on (user_id) WHERE status IN ('PLACED','PROCESSING') — only active orders
5. Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY for all (zero-downtime)

Note: CONCURRENTLY cannot be inside a transaction; Flyway must be configured for this.
exercises.starter_code:
-- V5__add_performance_indexes.sql
-- Performance indexes for orders table
-- Note: All use CONCURRENTLY for zero-downtime deployment
exercises.expected_solution:
-- V5__add_performance_indexes.sql
-- Note: CONCURRENTLY indexes cannot run inside a transaction.
-- Add to Flyway config: spring.flyway.mixed=true (to allow non-transactional statements)
-- Or: prefix with SQL comment-- flyway:executeInTransaction=false

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_user_id
    ON orders(user_id);

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_status_created
    ON orders(status, created_at DESC);
-- Good for: ORDER BY created_at DESC WHERE status = 'PLACED'

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_merchant_status
    ON orders(merchant_id, status);
-- Good for: WHERE merchant_id = ? AND status = ?

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_orders_active_user
    ON orders(user_id)
    WHERE status IN ('PLACED', 'PROCESSING');
-- Partial: only indexes active orders (small index, fast for open order queries)
-- Good for: WHERE user_id = ? AND status IN ('PLACED','PROCESSING')
hints: CONCURRENTLY: builds index without blocking writes
IF NOT EXISTS: idempotent — safe to re-run if migration was partially applied
Partial index WHERE: only indexes matching rows (smaller, faster)

exercises.title: Apply composite index leftmost prefix rule
exercises.scenario: Understanding which queries use an index is critical for effective indexing.
exercises.instructions:
Given this composite index:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_trades ON trades(user_id, symbol, executed_at);
```

For each query, state: (1) does the index help, (2) why/why not:

```sql
A: WHERE user_id = 'U001'
B: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND symbol = 'TCS'
C: WHERE symbol = 'TCS'
D: WHERE executed_at > '2024-01-01'
E: WHERE symbol = 'TCS' AND executed_at > '2024-01-01'
F: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND executed_at > '2024-01-01'
G: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND symbol = 'TCS' AND executed_at > '2024-01-01'
```
exercises.starter_code: // Leftmost prefix rule analysis for idx_trades(user_id, symbol, executed_at)
hints: Leftmost prefix: must include the first column to use the index
Skipping a middle column: index used for left portion only
Equality on all left columns + range on right = efficient index scan

exercises.title: Add Actuator metrics for HikariCP monitoring
exercises.scenario: Spring Actuator exposes HikariCP pool metrics for monitoring.
exercises.instructions:
Write the configuration and a test method for HikariCP monitoring:
1. pom.xml: actuator + micrometer-registry-prometheus dependencies
2. application.properties: expose health + metrics + prometheus
3. A @RestController /admin/pool endpoint that returns pool stats
4. Show example curl output for checking pool exhaustion
exercises.starter_code:
<!-- pom.xml snippets -->
# application.properties — Actuator config
// PoolMonitorController.java
// Example curl commands
exercises.expected_solution:
<!-- pom.xml:
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-actuator</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
    <artifactId>micrometer-registry-prometheus</artifactId>
    <scope>runtime</scope>
</dependency>
-->

# application.properties:
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,metrics,prometheus
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always
# Pool name for metrics:
spring.datasource.hikari.pool-name=Zerodha-Pool

// PoolMonitorController.java:
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.*;
import io.micrometer.core.instrument.MeterRegistry;
import io.micrometer.core.instrument.Gauge;
import java.util.*;

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/admin")
public class PoolMonitorController {
    private final MeterRegistry registry;
    PoolMonitorController(MeterRegistry r) { registry = r; }
    
    @GetMapping("/pool")
    public Map<String, Object> poolStats() {
        return Map.of(
            "total",   getMetric("hikaricp.connections"),
            "active",  getMetric("hikaricp.connections.active"),
            "idle",    getMetric("hikaricp.connections.idle"),
            "pending", getMetric("hikaricp.connections.pending"),
            "status",  getMetric("hikaricp.connections.pending") > 0 ? "EXHAUSTED" : "HEALTHY"
        );
    }
    private double getMetric(String name) {
        return registry.find(name).gauge() != null
            ? registry.find(name).gauge().value() : 0.0;
    }
}
// Example output (healthy):
// {"total":20,"active":7,"idle":13,"pending":0,"status":"HEALTHY"}

// Example output (exhausted):
// {"total":20,"active":20,"idle":0,"pending":47,"status":"EXHAUSTED"}

// Curl commands:
// curl localhost:8080/actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.pending
// curl localhost:8080/actuator/prometheus | grep hikari
// curl localhost:8080/admin/pool
hints: MeterRegistry: Micrometer's metric registry injected by Spring
hikaricp.connections.pending: key metric — if > 0, pool is exhausted
Prometheus: /actuator/prometheus → Grafana dashboard for pool trends

exercises.title: Design indexes for a trading platform query set
exercises.scenario: Given query patterns, design the minimal set of indexes needed.
exercises.instructions:
For the trades table (user_id, symbol, trade_type, price, qty, executed_at), write SQL for indexes that cover these queries efficiently:

1. `WHERE user_id = ? ORDER BY executed_at DESC` (portfolio history)
2. `WHERE symbol = ? AND executed_at > ? ORDER BY executed_at DESC` (market activity)
3. `WHERE user_id = ? AND symbol = ?` (position check)
4. `WHERE trade_type = 'BUY' AND executed_at > ?` (analytics — large but selective on type+date)
5. `SELECT symbol, SUM(qty) WHERE user_id = ?` (needs total qty per symbol)
exercises.starter_code:
-- Design indexes for Zerodha trades table
-- Minimize index count while maximizing query coverage
exercises.expected_solution:
-- INDEX DESIGN FOR 5 QUERY PATTERNS:

-- Query 1: WHERE user_id = ? ORDER BY executed_at DESC
-- Index: user_id + executed_at DESC for index-ordered retrieval
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_trades_user_time
    ON trades(user_id, executed_at DESC);
-- Also serves: WHERE user_id = ? AND symbol = ? (leftmost prefix: user_id)

-- Query 2: WHERE symbol = ? AND executed_at > ? ORDER BY executed_at DESC
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_trades_symbol_time
    ON trades(symbol, executed_at DESC);
-- symbol is leftmost for this access pattern

-- Query 3: WHERE user_id = ? AND symbol = ?
-- Already served by idx_trades_user_time (user_id leftmost)
-- But user_id+symbol more selective than user_id+time for this query
-- Add specific composite for best performance:
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_trades_user_symbol
    ON trades(user_id, symbol)
    INCLUDE (qty);  -- INCLUDE qty for covering index on Query 5!

-- Query 4: WHERE trade_type = 'BUY' AND executed_at > ?
-- Partial index for BUY only (50% of data):
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_trades_buy_time
    ON trades(executed_at)
    WHERE trade_type = 'BUY';
-- trade_type='BUY' is encoded in partial index, not as a column
-- Only half the rows indexed → smaller, faster

-- Query 5: SELECT symbol, SUM(qty) WHERE user_id = ?
-- Covered by idx_trades_user_symbol with INCLUDE(qty)
-- PostgreSQL can answer: SELECT symbol, qty WHERE user_id=? from index alone
-- → Index Only Scan (no heap access)

-- SUMMARY: 4 indexes cover 5 query patterns efficiently
-- Could combine 1+3 into idx_trades_user_symbol_time(user_id, symbol, executed_at)
-- but that index would be larger; depends on actual query frequency
hints: One index can serve multiple queries via leftmost prefix rule
INCLUDE: add SELECT columns for Index Only Scan on analytics queries
Partial index: WHERE trade_type='BUY' shrinks the index by 50%

exercises.title: Which index is used for each query?
exercises.scenario: Given multiple indexes, determine which one PostgreSQL uses for each query.
exercises.instructions:
Given these indexes on the orders table:
```sql
idx_a: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id);
idx_b: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id, status);
idx_c: CREATE INDEX ON orders(status, created_at);
idx_d: CREATE INDEX ON orders(user_id) WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';
```

For each query, which index (if any) is most likely used?
```
Q1: WHERE user_id = 'U001'
Q2: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE'
Q3: WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' ORDER BY created_at DESC
Q4: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE' (searching active orders only)
Q5: WHERE status = 'PENDING'
Q6: WHERE user_id = 'U001' ORDER BY status ASC
```
exercises.expected_solution:
Q1: WHERE user_id = 'U001'
  → idx_a OR idx_b (both start with user_id; PostgreSQL picks most efficient)
  idx_b may be preferred if smaller due to fewer heap fetches

Q2: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE'
  → idx_b (user_id, status) — two-column match, most specific
  → idx_d (partial: user_id WHERE status='ACTIVE') — also a good match
  → PostgreSQL compares cost; idx_d is smaller so likely chosen for this query

Q3: WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' ORDER BY created_at DESC
  → idx_c (status, created_at) — status is leftmost, created_at for ordering
  → Best option; supports both filter and order without sort step

Q4: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE' (active orders only)
  → idx_d (partial index for ACTIVE only) — smallest, fastest for this exact query
  → idx_b also works but indexes all statuses (larger)

Q5: WHERE status = 'PENDING'
  → idx_c (status, created_at) — status is leftmost
  → idx_d NOT used (partial: WHERE status='ACTIVE', not PENDING)

Q6: WHERE user_id = 'U001' ORDER BY status ASC
  → idx_b (user_id, status) — user_id filter + status already ordered in index
  → May avoid Sort step entirely → Index Scan in order

test_cases.expected:
Q1: WHERE user_id = 'U001'
  → idx_a OR idx_b (both start with user_id; PostgreSQL picks most efficient)
  idx_b may be preferred if smaller due to fewer heap fetches

Q2: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE'
  → idx_b (user_id, status) — two-column match, most specific
  → idx_d (partial: user_id WHERE status='ACTIVE') — also a good match
  → PostgreSQL compares cost; idx_d is smaller so likely chosen for this query

Q3: WHERE status = 'ACTIVE' ORDER BY created_at DESC
  → idx_c (status, created_at) — status is leftmost, created_at for ordering
  → Best option; supports both filter and order without sort step

Q4: WHERE user_id = 'U001' AND status = 'ACTIVE' (active orders only)
  → idx_d (partial index for ACTIVE only) — smallest, fastest for this exact query
  → idx_b also works but indexes all statuses (larger)

Q5: WHERE status = 'PENDING'
  → idx_c (status, created_at) — status is leftmost
  → idx_d NOT used (partial: WHERE status='ACTIVE', not PENDING)

Q6: WHERE user_id = 'U001' ORDER BY status ASC
  → idx_b (user_id, status) — user_id filter + status already ordered in index
  → May avoid Sort step entirely → Index Scan in order

hints: PostgreSQL picks the index with lowest estimated cost
Partial index only used when WHERE condition matches its partial WHERE
Ordered index scan: avoids Sort node when ORDER BY matches index column order

exercises.title: Covering index for analytics query
exercises.scenario: INCLUDE columns enable Index Only Scan — fastest possible query execution.
exercises.instructions:
Write a migration with a covering index for this analytics query:
```sql
SELECT symbol, SUM(qty * price) AS total_value
FROM trades
WHERE user_id = ?
  AND executed_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
GROUP BY symbol;
```

1. Identify which columns need to be in the index (leftmost prefix)
2. Identify which columns to INCLUDE (in the SELECT but not filter)
3. Write the CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY statement
4. Write the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output you'd expect (Index Only Scan)
5. Explain what Heap Fetches: 0 means
exercises.starter_code:
-- Covering index for trades analytics query
-- V6__add_covering_index_trades.sql
exercises.expected_solution:
-- Analysis:
-- WHERE columns (need to be INDEX key columns): user_id, executed_at
-- SELECT/GROUP BY columns (can go in INCLUDE): symbol, qty, price

-- V6__add_covering_index_trades.sql
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_trades_user_time_cover
    ON trades(user_id, executed_at)   -- key columns (leftmost for filter + range)
    INCLUDE (symbol, qty, price);     -- covering: all SELECT/GROUP BY columns

-- Expected EXPLAIN ANALYZE output after adding index:
-- HashAggregate (cost=... rows=15)
--   Group Key: symbol
--   -> Index Only Scan using idx_trades_user_time_cover on trades
--        Index Cond: (user_id='U001') AND
--                    (executed_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31')
--        Heap Fetches: 0    ← key indicator: all data from index!
-- Execution Time: 0.8ms    ← no heap access = very fast

-- Without covering index (regular index on user_id, executed_at):
-- Index Scan using idx_trades_user_time on trades
--   Heap Fetches: 8923    ← must access heap for symbol, qty, price
-- Execution Time: 45ms    ← slower (45× slower!)

-- Heap Fetches: 0 meaning:
-- PostgreSQL satisfied the ENTIRE query from the index structure alone.
-- No access to the actual table rows (heap) needed.
-- Index contains: (user_id, executed_at) [key] + (symbol, qty, price) [included]
-- All columns in SELECT and WHERE are in the index → zero heap reads → fastest possible
hints: INCLUDE columns: not part of index key, but stored in leaf pages for fast lookup
Index Only Scan: entire query answered from index — fastest execution plan
Heap Fetches: 0 = Index Only Scan — no need to access table rows

exercises.title: Complete HikariCP + slow query alert integration
exercises.scenario: Production monitoring: pool metrics + slow query detection via Actuator.
exercises.instructions:
Build a production monitoring setup:
1. HikariCP config with metrics exposed
2. MeterRegistry alert: if hikaricp.connections.pending > 5, log CRITICAL
3. Slow query interceptor using P6Spy or Spring's TraceInterceptor
4. application.properties: p6spy driver configuration
5. @SpringBootTest (integration test) verifying: pool connects, pool metrics available

All compiles and integration test passes.
exercises.expected_solution:
<!-- pom.xml:
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.github.gavlyukovskiy</groupId>
    <artifactId>p6spy-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    <version>1.9.0</version>
</dependency>
-->

# application.properties (with P6Spy wrapping PostgreSQL):
# P6Spy wraps the real driver to log SQL with timing:
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:p6spy:postgresql://localhost:5432/orders
spring.datasource.driver-class-name=com.p6spy.engine.spy.P6SpyDriver
decorator.datasource.p6spy.log-format=%(currentTime)|%(executionTime)|%(category)|%(sql)
decorator.datasource.p6spy.multiline=true
decorator.datasource.p6spy.logging=slf4j
# Log queries slower than 200ms:
decorator.datasource.p6spy.slow-query-threshold=200

spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=17
spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=2000
spring.datasource.hikari.pool-name=Orders-Pool

management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,metrics,prometheus

import io.micrometer.core.instrument.MeterRegistry;
import org.springframework.scheduling.annotation.Scheduled;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

@Service
public class HikariPoolAlertService {
    private final MeterRegistry registry;
    HikariPoolAlertService(MeterRegistry r) { registry = r; }
    
    @Scheduled(fixedRate = 30000)  // every 30 seconds
    public void checkPoolHealth() {
        double pending = getValue("hikaricp.connections.pending");
        double active  = getValue("hikaricp.connections.active");
        double total   = getValue("hikaricp.connections");
        
        if (pending > 5) {
            System.err.printf("[CRITICAL] Pool exhausted! pending=%.0f active=%.0f/%0.f%n",
                pending, active, total);
            // In production: trigger PagerDuty / Slack alert
        } else if (active / total > 0.85) {
            System.out.printf("[WARN] Pool >85%% utilized: active=%.0f/%.0f%n", active, total);
        }
    }
    private double getValue(String m) {
        var g = registry.find(m).gauge();
        return g != null ? g.value() : 0;
    }
}

import org.springframework.boot.test.context.SpringBootTest;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import javax.sql.DataSource;
import java.sql.Connection;
import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;
import org.junit.jupiter.api.*;

@SpringBootTest
class PoolIntegrationTest {
    @Autowired DataSource dataSource;
    @Autowired io.micrometer.core.instrument.MeterRegistry registry;
    
    @Test void pool_canConnect() throws Exception {
        try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection()) {
            assertTrue(conn.isValid(2));
        }
    }
    @Test void pool_metricsAvailable() {
        var gauge = registry.find("hikaricp.connections").gauge();
        assertNotNull(gauge, "HikariCP metrics should be registered");
        assertTrue(gauge.value() > 0, "Pool should have connections");
    }
}
hints: P6Spy: wraps the JDBC driver, logs SQL with timing before passing to real driver
slow-query-threshold=200: P6Spy logs queries > 200ms to SLOW_QUERY logger
@Scheduled alert: run every 30s, check pending > threshold
